era_context: The mid-18th century was dominated by the War of the Austrian Succession (1740–1748), which pitted France, Prussia, and Spain against Austria, Britain, and the Dutch Republic across European, Indian, and North American theatres. The Enlightenment was reshaping intellectual life in Europe, while the Qing Dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor presided over a prosperous and expanding Chinese empire. In India, the Mughal Empire's authority continued to fragment as the Marathas, Nizam of Hyderabad, and European trading companies competed for regional dominance. The Atlantic slave trade was at its height, and Britain's North American colonies were growing rapidly.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (interior kingdoms such as Ashanti, Dahomey, and Oyo are poorly documented in European-language sources for this year)
Southeast Asia (Burmese, Siamese, and Vietnamese court records underrepresented in English-language historiography)
Pacific Islands and Oceania (pre-European-contact oral traditions)

events.title: Battle of Culloden ends the Jacobite Rising
events.description: On 16 April 1746, government forces under the Duke of Cumberland decisively defeated the Jacobite army of Charles Edward Stuart ('Bonnie Prince Charlie') at Culloden Moor near Inverness. The battle lasted barely an hour, with the Jacobite force of roughly 5,000–6,000 men destroyed by approximately 7,000–8,000 well-supplied regulars employing disciplined musket volleys and artillery. Between 1,500 and 2,000 Jacobites were killed on the field and in the brutal pursuit that followed, compared to roughly 50 government dead. Culloden was the last pitched battle fought on British soil and ended any realistic prospect of a Stuart restoration. The defeat triggered severe reprisals against Highland Scottish society, including systematic disarmament, the banning of Highland dress, and the dismantling of the clan system.
key_figures: Charles Edward Stuart (Jacobite claimant, 'Bonnie Prince Charlie')
Prince William Augustus, Duke of Cumberland (government commander)
Lord George Murray (Jacobite lieutenant-general)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts including the Duke of Cumberland's dispatches to George II and the Lyon in Mourning (collected testimonies by Bishop Robert Forbes, compiled 1747–1775)
Stuart Reid, Culloden 1746: Battlefield Britain (2005); Trevor Royle, Culloden: Scotland's Last Battle and the Forging of the British Empire (2016)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by both government and Jacobite sources. Date, location, and outcome are not in dispute.

events.title: Flight and escape of Bonnie Prince Charlie
events.description: After Culloden, Charles Edward Stuart spent five months as a fugitive in the Scottish Highlands and Western Isles, evading government patrols despite a £30,000 reward for his capture. Aided by loyal supporters including Flora MacDonald, who famously disguised him as her maid 'Betty Burke' for a crossing from the Outer Hebrides to Skye in June, Charles eventually escaped to France on the French frigate L'Heureux on 20 September 1746. His flight became central to Jacobite romantic mythology but also demonstrated the depth of Highland loyalty and the limits of government control in remote Scotland. Charles would never return to Britain.
key_figures: Charles Edward Stuart (fugitive Jacobite claimant)
Flora MacDonald (Jacobite sympathiser who aided his escape)
Captain Michael Sheridan (aboard L'Heureux)

sources.name: The Lyon in Mourning, compiled by Bishop Robert Forbes (1747–1775), containing first-hand accounts of the escape
Fitzroy Maclean, Bonnie Prince Charlie (1988)

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary and near-contemporary accounts document the escape route in detail. Some individual episodes are embellished in later tradition.

events.title: Post-Culloden repression and the Disarming Act
events.description: Following Culloden, the British government carried out systematic repression of Highland Scottish culture and society. Cumberland's forces conducted punitive raids, burning homes, seizing cattle, and executing or transporting suspected Jacobites. Parliament passed the Act of Proscription (Disarming Act) on 1 August 1746, which prohibited the carrying of arms, the wearing of Highland dress including tartan and kilts, and restricted the heritable jurisdictions of clan chiefs. These measures aimed to dismantle the clan-based military society that had enabled the Jacobite risings. The legislation fundamentally transformed Highland life and accelerated the integration of Scotland into a unified British state.
key_figures: Duke of Cumberland (military commander enforcing repression)
King George II (British monarch)

sources.name: Act of Proscription 1746 (19 Geo. II, c.39), Parliamentary records
T.M. Devine, The Scottish Nation: A Modern History (2006)

events.certainty_note: Legislation is documented in Parliamentary archives. The scope and enforcement of reprisals are well attested in contemporary correspondence and later testimonies.

events.title: Battle of Rocoux
events.description: On 11 October 1746, a French army of approximately 120,000 men under Marshal Maurice de Saxe defeated an Allied force of roughly 80,000 Austrians, Dutch, and British at Rocoux (Rocour), near Liege. Saxe employed a concentrated assault on the Allied left, held by the Dutch, which collapsed under pressure. The Allies suffered approximately 5,000 casualties and retreated in good order, but the victory gave France effective control of the Austrian Netherlands. The battle demonstrated Saxe's mastery of 18th-century warfare and further undermined the Allied position in the Low Countries.
key_figures: Maurice de Saxe (Marshal of France, French commander)
Charles of Lorraine (Allied commander)
Duke of Cumberland (British contingent commander, absent due to recall to face the Jacobite threat)

sources.name: Maurice de Saxe, Mes Reveries (published posthumously 1757) and contemporary French army dispatches
Reed Browning, The War of the Austrian Succession (1993)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in French, Austrian, British, and Dutch military records.

events.title: French capture of Brussels
events.description: In February 1746, French forces under Marshal de Saxe captured Brussels, the administrative capital of the Austrian Netherlands, following a brief siege. The city fell on 20 February after its garrison proved insufficient to mount a prolonged defence. The capture of Brussels was part of France's broader campaign to conquer the Austrian Netherlands during the War of the Austrian Succession, a strategy intended to use territorial gains as bargaining leverage in peace negotiations. By the end of 1746, most major cities in the Austrian Netherlands were under French control.
key_figures: Maurice de Saxe (Marshal of France)
Maria Theresa (Archduchess of Austria, whose territory was being overrun)

sources.name: French military correspondence and contemporary gazettes
Reed Browning, The War of the Austrian Succession (1993)

events.certainty_note: Date and circumstances are well documented in military and diplomatic records.

events.title: Battle of Piacenza
events.region: Northern Italy (Duchy of Parma and Piacenza)
events.description: On 16 June 1746, an Austrian army under Prince von Liechtenstein defeated a combined Franco-Spanish force at Piacenza in northern Italy. The battle was the most significant engagement of the Italian theatre of the War of the Austrian Succession in 1746. The Franco-Spanish army suffered heavy casualties and was forced to retreat, beginning the unravelling of the Bourbon position in northern Italy. The Austrian victory at Piacenza, combined with subsequent operations, eventually drove the French and Spanish from most of the Po Valley by late 1746.
key_figures: Prince Joseph Wenzel von Liechtenstein (Austrian commander)
Jean-Baptiste Desmarets, Marquis de Maillebois (French commander)

sources.name: Austrian military dispatches and contemporary campaign narratives
Reed Browning, The War of the Austrian Succession (1993)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in Austrian, French, and Spanish military records.

events.title: Death of Philip V of Spain; accession of Ferdinand VI
events.description: Philip V, the first Bourbon King of Spain, died on 9 July 1746 at the age of 62 after a reign of nearly 46 years, one of the longest in Spanish history. He was succeeded by his son Ferdinand VI, who immediately signalled a shift toward peace and neutrality. Ferdinand's reign would be characterised by efforts to disengage Spain from costly continental wars, reform royal finances, and rebuild the Spanish navy. The change of monarch contributed to Spain's growing willingness to negotiate an end to the War of the Austrian Succession.
key_figures: Philip V (King of Spain, deceased)
Ferdinand VI (new King of Spain)
Barbara of Braganza (Queen consort, influential on Ferdinand)

sources.name: Spanish court records and contemporary diplomatic correspondence
John Lynch, Bourbon Spain, 1700–1808 (1989)

events.certainty_note: Date of death and succession are firmly established in Spanish royal and diplomatic records.

events.title: Duc d'Anville expedition disaster
events.region: Atlantic Ocean / Nova Scotia, North America
events.description: In June 1746, France dispatched a massive naval expedition of approximately 64 ships and 11,000 men under the Duc d'Anville to recapture Louisbourg and attack British positions in Nova Scotia and New England. The expedition was devastated by storms, disease (typhus and scurvy), and logistical failures during the Atlantic crossing. By the time the remnants reached Chebucto (modern Halifax) in September, thousands had died and d'Anville himself succumbed to illness on 27 September. His successor, d'Estournelle, committed suicide days later. The surviving force attempted an assault on Annapolis Royal but abandoned the campaign. The disaster was one of the greatest French naval catastrophes of the 18th century and left New France vulnerable.
key_figures: Jean-Baptiste de Roye de La Rochefoucauld, Duc d'Anville (expedition commander, died of illness)
Marquis d'Estournelle (successor commander, committed suicide)
Jonquiere (third commander, led retreat)

sources.name: French naval records and dispatches; James S. Pritchard, Anatomy of a Naval Disaster: The 1746 French Expedition to North America (1995)
James Pritchard, Anatomy of a Naval Disaster (1995)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in French naval archives and British colonial reports.

events.title: French capture of Madras
events.region: South India — Coromandel Coast (modern Chennai, Tamil Nadu)
events.description: On 21 September 1746, French forces under Bertrand-Francois Mahe de La Bourdonnais, Governor of the Ile de France (Mauritius), captured the British East India Company settlement of Madras (Fort St. George) after a brief siege. La Bourdonnais had defeated a British naval squadron and landed troops who, combined with a force sent by Joseph Francois Dupleix from Pondicherry, overwhelmed the small British garrison. The capture of Madras was a major blow to British prestige in India and a pivotal moment in the First Carnatic War. However, La Bourdonnais and Dupleix quarrelled over the terms — La Bourdonnais had promised to ransom the city back to Britain, while Dupleix wished to retain it. Madras would be returned to Britain in the 1748 Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle.
key_figures: Bertrand-Francois Mahe de La Bourdonnais (Governor of Ile de France, French naval commander)
Joseph Francois Dupleix (Governor-General of French India)
Nicholas Morse (British Governor of Madras, surrendered)

sources.name: East India Company records and French colonial correspondence from Pondicherry
G.B. Malleson, History of the French in India (1868); Sanjay Subrahmanyam, Penumbral Visions: Making Polities in Early Modern South India (2001)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in both British and French East India Company archives. Exact date of surrender is attested in multiple sources.

events.title: Nizam ul-Mulk's attempts to resist Maratha expansion
events.description: In 1746, Nizam ul-Mulk Asaf Jah I, the aging ruler of Hyderabad, continued his complex diplomacy to resist the growing power of the Maratha Confederacy in the Deccan. The Marathas under Peshwa Balaji Baji Rao were steadily expanding their tributary demands and territorial control across central India. The Nizam attempted to play European powers and other Indian rulers against Maratha interests, but his declining health and the fragmentation of Mughal authority limited his effectiveness. His death in 1748 would trigger a succession crisis that the Marathas and European companies would exploit.
key_figures: Nizam ul-Mulk Asaf Jah I (ruler of Hyderabad)
Balaji Baji Rao (Peshwa of the Maratha Confederacy)

sources.name: Mughal court records and Maratha Peshwa daftar (administrative records)
Stewart Gordon, The Marathas 1600–1818 (1993)

events.certainty_note: The broad dynamics are well established, but specific diplomatic events in 1746 are less precisely dated than the European theatre.

events.title: College of New Jersey (later Princeton University) chartered
events.region: Province of New Jersey, British North America
events.description: On 22 October 1746, the College of New Jersey was chartered by Royal Governor John Hamilton, making it the fourth college established in British North America. Founded by New Light Presbyterians including Jonathan Dickinson, the college reflected the religious and educational ferment of the Great Awakening. It aimed to train ministers and civic leaders in a broadly evangelical but intellectually rigorous tradition. The college would later relocate to Princeton in 1756 and eventually become Princeton University, one of the most influential institutions in American intellectual and political life.
key_figures: Jonathan Dickinson (first president)
Aaron Burr Sr. (second president)
John Hamilton (acting Governor of New Jersey, granted the charter)

sources.name: Charter of the College of New Jersey (22 October 1746), held in Princeton University archives
Thomas Jefferson Wertenbaker, Princeton 1746–1896 (1946)

events.certainty_note: The charter document survives and is precisely dated.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's First Jinchuan Campaign begins
events.description: In 1746, the Qianlong Emperor initiated the first military campaign against the Jinchuan Tibetan chieftains in the mountainous border region of western Sichuan. The conflict arose from succession disputes among local tusi (native chieftains) that threatened Qing control over a strategically important frontier zone. The campaign proved far more difficult than anticipated, as the stone fortress (diaolou) defences of the Jinchuan proved nearly impervious to conventional assault in the rugged terrain. The war would drag on until 1749, consuming enormous resources and prompting soul-searching about Qing military capabilities on the southwestern frontier.
key_figures: Qianlong Emperor (Qing ruler)
Sa-la-er (Jinchuan chieftain)
Zhang Guang-si (Qing military commander)

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty), Qianlong reign section
Dai Yingcong, The Sichuan Frontier and Tibet: Imperial Strategy in the Early Qing (2009)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Qing imperial records, though the exact sequence of early engagements in 1746 is sometimes grouped with 1747 in secondary sources.

events.title: Ahmad Shah Durrani consolidates the Durrani Empire
events.description: By 1746, Ahmad Shah Durrani, who had founded the Durrani Empire in 1747 after Nader Shah's assassination, was still serving as a commander in Nader Shah's increasingly unstable empire. However, the Afsharid state under Nader Shah was visibly disintegrating due to the shah's growing paranoia, punitive taxation, and revolts across Persia. Ahmad Khan (as he was then known) commanded a contingent of Abdali Afghans and was positioning himself for independence. The instability of 1746 Persia set the stage for the rapid collapse and fragmentation that followed Nader Shah's assassination the following year.
key_figures: Ahmad Khan Abdali (later Ahmad Shah Durrani, Afghan commander under Nader Shah)
Nader Shah (Afsharid ruler of Persia, increasingly despotic)

sources.name: Tarikh-i Ahmad Shahi by Mahmud al-Husayni (near-contemporary chronicle of Ahmad Shah's career)
Michael Axworthy, The Sword of Persia: Nader Shah from Tribal Warrior to Conquering Tyrant (2006)

events.certainty_note: The political dynamics of Nader Shah's declining years are well established; the specific positioning of Ahmad Khan in 1746 is reconstructed from later chronicles.

events.title: Anti-Jewish pogroms and expulsion in Moravia considered
events.description: In 1744–1745, Maria Theresa had ordered the expulsion of Jews from Prague and Bohemia, a decree that was partially enforced amid widespread hardship. By 1746, international diplomatic pressure — particularly from Britain, the Dutch Republic, and the Ottoman Empire, all of whom had commercial interests tied to Jewish merchants — along with petitions from Bohemian estates highlighting economic damage, led to a gradual reversal. Maria Theresa allowed Jews to return to Prague beginning in 1748, though under restrictive conditions. The episode illustrated both the vulnerability of Jewish communities to royal caprice and the limits imposed by economic interdependence.
key_figures: Maria Theresa (Archduchess of Austria and Queen of Bohemia)
Wolf Wertheimer (Court Jew who lobbied for reversal)

sources.name: Habsburg state papers and diplomatic correspondence; community records (pinkas) of Prague's Jewish community
Michael K. Silber, 'From Tolerated Aliens to Citizen-Soldiers' in Rethinking European Jewish History (2008)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Habsburg administrative records and Jewish communal archives. The diplomatic dimensions are attested in multiple countries' correspondence.

events.title: Battle of Negapatam (naval engagement off Coromandel coast)
events.description: On 6 July 1746, a British naval squadron under Commodore Edward Peyton engaged a French squadron under Bertrand-Francois Mahe de La Bourdonnais off Negapatam (Nagapattinam) on the southeastern coast of India. The engagement was inconclusive but strategically damaging to British interests: Peyton withdrew his damaged ships to the safety of Hooghly, effectively ceding naval superiority in the Bay of Bengal to La Bourdonnais. This French naval dominance enabled the subsequent capture of Madras in September 1746 and demonstrated the critical importance of sea power in the emerging Anglo-French contest for India.
key_figures: Edward Peyton (British Commodore)
Bertrand-Francois Mahe de La Bourdonnais (French commander)

sources.name: British Admiralty records and East India Company naval correspondence
H.V. Bowen, War and British Society 1688–1815 (1998)

events.certainty_note: Documented in British and French naval records.

events.title: Genoa revolts against Austrian occupation
events.description: On 5 December 1746, the people of Genoa rose in revolt against the Austrian occupation force that had controlled the city since September. According to tradition, the uprising was sparked when Austrian soldiers tried to move a mortar through the working-class neighbourhood of Portoria and a boy named Balilla threw a stone at them. Within days, the Genoese drove the Austrian garrison out of the city. The revolt was remarkable as a successful urban popular insurrection against a professional army and became a foundational event in Genoese and later Italian nationalist memory.
key_figures: Giovan Battista Perasso ('Balilla', legendary boy who sparked the revolt)
Marquis Botta Adorno (Austrian military governor, expelled)

sources.name: Contemporary Genoese chronicles and Austrian military reports
Gregory Hanlon, The Twilight of a Military Tradition: Italian Aristocrats and European Conflicts 1560–1800 (1998)

events.certainty_note: The revolt is well documented. The story of Balilla specifically is traditional and may be embellished, though the popular uprising itself is not in dispute.

events.title: Denis Diderot publishes Pensees philosophiques
events.description: In 1746, Denis Diderot published his first major philosophical work, Pensees philosophiques (Philosophical Thoughts), a collection of aphorisms challenging religious orthodoxy and advancing deist and sceptical arguments. The work was condemned by the Parlement of Paris on 7 July 1746 and publicly burned. Despite this censorship, the book circulated widely and established Diderot as a bold Enlightenment thinker. The controversy foreshadowed his later, more ambitious project — the Encyclopedie — and his repeated conflicts with French censorship authorities.
key_figures: Denis Diderot (philosopher and author)

sources.name: Denis Diderot, Pensees philosophiques (1746) — the work itself; Parlement de Paris condemnation records
Arthur M. Wilson, Diderot (1972)

events.certainty_note: Publication and condemnation are documented in Parlement records and contemporary correspondence.

events.title: Renewed slave trade expansion on the West African coast
events.region: West Africa (Gold Coast, Slave Coast, Bight of Biafra)
events.description: The mid-1740s represented a peak period in the transatlantic slave trade. In 1746, British, French, Portuguese, and Dutch traders continued to operate extensively along the West African coast, with the largest volumes coming from the Bight of Benin and the Gold Coast. The Dahomey kingdom under King Tegbesu was actively involved in slave raiding and trading with European factors at Ouidah (Whydah). British slave-trading ports like Liverpool and Bristol dispatched hundreds of voyages. An estimated 80,000 or more enslaved Africans were transported across the Atlantic in this year, primarily to Caribbean sugar plantations and the American colonies.
key_figures: Tegbesu (King of Dahomey)

sources.name: Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database (slavevoyages.org), compiled from shipping records, port books, and colonial archives
David Eltis, The Rise of African Slavery in the Americas (2000); Robin Law, Ouidah: The Social History of a West African Slaving Port (2004)

events.certainty_note: Volume estimates are derived from the Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database, which aggregates shipping records. Individual voyage data for 1746 is well documented, though total figures carry some uncertainty.

events.title: Leonhard Euler publishes Nova theoria lucis et colorum
events.description: In 1746, the Swiss mathematician Leonhard Euler, working at the Berlin Academy under Frederick the Great, published his Nova theoria lucis et colorum (New Theory of Light and Colours), which advanced a wave theory of light in opposition to Newton's corpuscular theory. Euler argued that light consisted of vibrations in an elastic medium (the ether) and related colour to wavelength. Although the wave-particle debate would not be resolved until quantum mechanics in the 20th century, Euler's work was an important contribution to optics and represented one of the most serious 18th-century challenges to Newtonian orthodoxy.
key_figures: Leonhard Euler (mathematician and physicist)

sources.name: Leonhard Euler, 'Nova theoria lucis et colorum,' Opuscula varii argumenti (1746)
Ronald Calinger, Leonhard Euler: Mathematical Genius in the Enlightenment (2016)

events.certainty_note: Publication date and contents are documented in Berlin Academy records.

events.title: Tokugawa Japan under Shogun Ieshige
events.description: In 1746, Japan was governed by Tokugawa Ieshige, the ninth Tokugawa shogun, who had acceded in 1745. Ieshige suffered from a severe speech impediment and relied heavily on his chamberlain Ooka Tadamitsu to interpret his wishes, leading to a concentration of power among favourites. The Kyoho famine of 1732 and subsequent fiscal strains had prompted the shogunate under Ieshige's predecessor Yoshimune to enact economic reforms, but Ieshige's rule saw a relaxation of these policies. Japan remained in its period of sakoku (closed country), with foreign trade restricted to the Dutch at Dejima and limited Chinese commerce at Nagasaki.
key_figures: Tokugawa Ieshige (Shogun of Japan)
Ooka Tadamitsu (chamberlain and interpreter)

sources.name: Tokugawa jikki (Veritable Records of the Tokugawa), compiled in the early 19th century from bakufu archives
Conrad Totman, Early Modern Japan (1993)

events.certainty_note: Tokugawa administrative records are detailed and well preserved, though Ieshige's reign is less thoroughly studied than some predecessors.

disconfirming_evidence: The story of 'Balilla' sparking the Genoese revolt (1746-016) is likely legendary or at least heavily embellished. While the December 1746 revolt itself is well documented, the specific anecdote of a boy throwing a stone was popularised in 19th-century Italian nationalist historiography and may be apocryphal. The Battle of Culloden casualty figures (1746-001) vary across sources; some Jacobite accounts give lower government casualties to emphasise the one-sided nature of the defeat, while government sources sometimes inflate Jacobite losses. The traditional narrative of Culloden as an 'England vs. Scotland' conflict is misleading — significant numbers of Scots fought on the government side, and the Jacobite army included English, Irish, and French troops. For the d'Anville expedition (1746-008), the death toll from disease is estimated variably between 2,000 and 8,000 depending on the source, and d'Anville's precise cause of death (stroke vs. illness vs. possible suicide) remains debated.
historiographic_note: 1746 is richly documented from European perspectives, with extensive state papers, military dispatches, parliamentary records, and newspaper coverage available from Britain, France, Austria, and Spain. Indian events are documented primarily through European East India Company records, which carry colonial biases and focus on coastal trading enclaves rather than interior polities. Qing Chinese sources (Shilu, memorials) are detailed but primarily reflect the imperial court's perspective. West African events are documented almost entirely through European trading records and are heavily filtered through the lens of the slave trade. Japanese sources are extensive but internally focused due to sakoku. The War of the Austrian Succession dominates European historiography for this year, potentially overshadowing significant developments elsewhere.

graph_edges.note: Culloden was the decisive conclusion of the 1745 Jacobite Rising begun with Charles Edward Stuart's landing in Scotland
Post-Culloden repression and the Disarming Act were direct consequences of the Jacobite defeat
French naval superiority after Negapatam enabled the capture of Madras
Madras was returned to Britain under the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle in 1748
French conquests in the Austrian Netherlands were leveraged in the 1748 peace negotiations
Ferdinand VI's preference for peace contributed to Spain's acceptance of the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle
Instability of Nader Shah's empire in 1746 set the stage for his assassination and Ahmad Shah Durrani's rise in 1747
Diderot's Pensees philosophiques established his reputation and led to his editorship of the Encyclopedie
Austrian occupation of Genoa resulted from their Italian campaign successes including Piacenza
The First Jinchuan Campaign dragged on until a negotiated settlement in 1749

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
